summary: Turnkey Chromium credential-extraction toolkit published as a library
details: The package's advertised, documented public API extracts saved credentials from Chromium-based browsers (Chrome, Edge, Brave). It reads each browser's Local State, decrypts the DPAPI-protected master key, attaches a debugger to the running browser process to recover the v20 App-Bound AES key (via a native addon), then decrypts the Login Data and Cookies SQLite databases and writes plaintext passwords to./<browser>_passwords/<profile>.txt and cookies in Netscape format to./<browser>_cookies/<profile>.txt. No network exfiltration is performed and the install lifecycle does only a node-gyp rebuild of the shipped native addon — installing or requiring the package does not, by itself, harm the installer. Separately, src/license.js gates the public API by fetching a newline-delimited allowlist of license keys from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/tabo1337/SaatPCBKod/refs/heads/main/key.txt — a mutable `main` branch on a GitHub account different from the package author (`ibrahim1337` vs `tabo1337`), giving that third party unilateral control over which keys are accepted. No installer data is sent outward and no fetched code is executed; the third-party gate is an unusual licensing arrangement, not an exfil or RCE channel. The package is functionally a turnkey infostealer toolkit shipped as a library; flagging for human/registry-policy review is appropriate even though no covert installer-side attack mechanism is present.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
